CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear logical flow (Introduction -> Preliminaries/Setup -> Method (Section 3 BPG) -> Experiments -> Conclusion), and the core method is presented with algorithms and pseudo-code (Algorithm 1, Algorithm 2 in Appendix A). The main contribution—the batch off‑policy policy gradient (BPG) for noisy/expensive rewards—is described early but would benefit from a concise, explicit bulletized statement of contributions in the Introduction. Notation is generally consistent and defined in Section 2, and the use of equations and algorithmic steps is helpful; however there are presentation issues (repeated/garbled paragraphs and LaTeX artifacts in the addendum and end of the provided text) and a few minor grammatical/formatting slips that reduce polish. Figures/tables are referenced and appear to support the narrative, though their absence in the provided text makes full assessment impossible. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear structure (Introduction, Related Work, Preliminaries, Method, Experiments, Conclusion) and the main contribution — an off‑policy batch policy gradient method (BPG) for training Seq2Seq chatbots — is described early (Intro & paragraph before Section 2). Notation is largely consistent and defined (Sec. 2.1–2.3 and the MDP formalism), and Algorithm 1 and Appendix A give concrete procedural details. Presentation issues are minor but notable: several duplicated/garbled lines appear in the addendum (B.2 and the end of the file), and some phrasing/formatting choices (e.g. many inline citations and a few awkward sentences) reduce readability in parts. Figures/tables are referenced and appear to be informative from the text, but the repeated text blocks and small grammatical/typographical errors distract from an otherwise clear exposition.

The main contribution—the batch off‑policy policy gradient (BPG) for noisy/expensive rewards—is described early but would benefit from a concise, explicit bulletized statement of contributions in the Introduction. Notation is generally consistent and defined in Section 2, and the use of equations and algorithmic steps is helpful; however there are presentation issues (repeated/garbled paragraphs and LaTeX artifacts in the addendum and end of the provided text) and a few minor grammatical/formatting slips that reduce polish. Figures/tables are referenced and appear to support the narrative, though their absence in the provided text makes full assessment impossible.

Notation is largely consistent and defined (Sec. 2.1–2.3 and the MDP formalism), and Algorithm 1 and Appendix A give concrete procedural details. Presentation issues are minor but notable: several duplicated/garbled lines appear in the addendum (B.2 and the end of the file), and some phrasing/formatting choices (e.g. many inline citations and a few awkward sentences) reduce readability in parts. Figures/tables are referenced and appear to be informative from the text, but the repeated text blocks and small grammatical/typographical errors distract from an otherwise clear exposition.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a practical, well-motivated adaptation of existing off-policy policy-gradient ideas to the Seq2Seq chatbot setting (the BPG algorithm in Sec. 3), emphasizing batch learning, importance sampling and λ-returns computed from logged future actions. This is a clear and relevant application-level contribution that the authors differentiate from prior on-policy Seq2Seq RL work (Ranzato et al., Bahdanau et al., Williams & Zweig) and from Degris et al. (Sec. 3). However, most core techniques (importance weighting, λ-returns, GTD(λ), batch updates, clipping) are known and the paper mainly composes and adapts them to this domain rather than introducing fundamentally new theory, so the novelty is moderate (incremental but useful). | B(3): The paper adapts and combines existing off-policy RL ideas to Seq2Seq chatbots rather than proposing a fundamentally new algorithm: Section 3 derives a batch policy-gradient following Degris et al. (2012) and uses importance sampling ρ, λ-returns (Eq.3) and GTD(λ) for value estimation (Appendix A). Its novelty lies in applying an off-policy, batch RL formulation to noisy, costly human rewards in NLP and practical choices (clipping, fixing lower LSTM layer), which contrasts with prior on-policy/on-line NLP work (Ranzato et al., Bahdanau et al., Li et al., Williams & Zweig) but is an incremental, non-trivial engineering/setting-level contribution rather than a wholly new method.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a practical, well-motivated adaptation of existing off-policy policy-gradient ideas to the Seq2Seq chatbot setting (the BPG algorithm in Sec. 3), emphasizing batch learning, importance sampling and λ-returns computed from logged future actions. This is a clear and relevant application-level contribution that the authors differentiate from prior on-policy Seq2Seq RL work (Ranzato et al., Bahdanau et al., Williams & Zweig) and from Degris et al. (Sec. 3). However, most core techniques (importance weighting, λ-returns, GTD(λ), batch updates, clipping) are known and the paper mainly composes and adapts them to this domain rather than introducing fundamentally new theory, so the novelty is moderate (incremental but useful).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper adapts and combines existing off-policy RL ideas to Seq2Seq chatbots rather than proposing a fundamentally new algorithm: Section 3 derives a batch policy-gradient following Degris et al. (2012) and uses importance sampling ρ, λ-returns (Eq.3) and GTD(λ) for value estimation (Appendix A). Its novelty lies in applying an off-policy, batch RL formulation to noisy, costly human rewards in NLP and practical choices (clipping, fixing lower LSTM layer), which contrasts with prior on-policy/on-line NLP work (Ranzato et al., Bahdanau et al., Li et al., Williams & Zweig) but is an incremental, non-trivial engineering/setting-level contribution rather than a wholly new method.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported: the policy-gradient derivation (Eqns. 2–3) follows standard off‑policy reasoning and cites Degris et al. for the approximation, and experiments compare BPG vs on‑policy / no‑IS baselines showing empirical gains. The algorithmic choices (importance weighting, λ‑returns, clipping) and implementation details are reasonable and described. Concerns are limited technical gaps: the GTD(λ) estimator is adapted to a non‑linear (logistic) value parametrisation without theoretical justification (Maei’s results target linear/GTD settings), and relying on a learned behaviour q(a|s) can introduce bias and high IS variance (mitigated heuristically by clipping). Experimental evidence is somewhat limited (small labelled set, only marginal significance in one bot) but does not invalidate the main technical claims. | B(4): The paper presents a coherent off-policy batch policy-gradient (BPG) method with correct high-level derivations (eqs. (2),(3)), sensible use of importance sampling, λ-returns, and a practical GTD(λ) estimator (Appendix A). Empirical methodology includes reasonable baselines (ML, on-line OPG, no-IS) and paired statistical tests on AMT labels, though real-data significance is weak (only one bot significant at 10%) and experimental label sizes are small. Technical caveats not fully addressed include reliance on a learned behaviour policy q(a|s) (support mismatch and bias of IS), the pragmatic freezing of the bottom LSTM (treating φ(s) as part of the environment) which is an approximation, and lack of theoretical convergence guarantees with the function approximation/ clipping used. Overall the methods and derivations are sound with minor practical/assumption limitations that do not invalidate the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported: the policy-gradient derivation (Eqns. 2–3) follows standard off‑policy reasoning and cites Degris et al. for the approximation, and experiments compare BPG vs on‑policy / no‑IS baselines showing empirical gains. The algorithmic choices (importance weighting, λ‑returns, clipping) and implementation details are reasonable and described. Concerns are limited technical gaps: the GTD(λ) estimator is adapted to a non‑linear (logistic) value parametrisation without theoretical justification (Maei’s results target linear/GTD settings), and relying on a learned behaviour q(a|s) can introduce bias and high IS variance (mitigated heuristically by clipping). Experimental evidence is somewhat limited (small labelled set, only marginal significance in one bot) but does not invalidate the main technical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a coherent off-policy batch policy-gradient (BPG) method with correct high-level derivations (eqs. (2),(3)), sensible use of importance sampling, λ-returns, and a practical GTD(λ) estimator (Appendix A). Empirical methodology includes reasonable baselines (ML, on-line OPG, no-IS) and paired statistical tests on AMT labels, though real-data significance is weak (only one bot significant at 10%) and experimental label sizes are small. Technical caveats not fully addressed include reliance on a learned behaviour policy q(a|s) (support mismatch and bias of IS), the pragmatic freezing of the bottom LSTM (treating φ(s) as part of the environment) which is an approximation, and lack of theoretical convergence guarantees with the function approximation/ clipping used. Overall the methods and derivations are sound with minor practical/assumption limitations that do not invalidate the main conclusions.
